claim: Agents require composable, stateful computers with instant startup, dynamic resources, and pause/resume capabilities—not disposable code execution boxes. Design sandboxes to be long-running and resumable like laptops, not preemptable containers.
actionable: When architecting agent compute infrastructure, prioritize stateful snapshots, pause/resume, and machine persistence over ephemeral isolation. Combine Lambda speed with EC2 longevity.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: Bare-metal architecture with custom schedulers (not Kubernetes) enables critical sandbox performance: 60ms single spin-up, 50k concurrent in 75 seconds, instant local NVMe snapshots. Kubernetes and Nomad cannot achieve this for agent workloads.
actionable: For high-throughput agent workloads, evaluate bare-metal providers with custom schedulers. Kubernetes adds network latency between EBS/storage and compute that breaks agent performance requirements.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: Agent workloads split into two usage patterns: (1) long-running background agents (follow-the-sun, predictable daily cycles like human developers), and (2) RL/eval workloads (highly spiky, 0% to 100,000+ CPUs instantly, unpredictable timing). These require different capacity planning and commitment strategies.
actionable: Separate compute capacity and pricing models for background agents vs. RL workloads. Use overprovisioning or just-in-time compute for RL spikes; predictable scaling for long-running agents.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: RL/eval workload distribution is not geographically predictable—users fire jobs at midnight before sleeping, creating sudden 100x spikes. This breaks Cloudflare-style geo-load-balancing tactics that rely on predictable follow-the-sun patterns.
actionable: For RL-heavy platforms, do not rely on geo-distribution alone for utilization optimization. Build reserved capacity or dynamic inter-region scheduling for unpredictable spike workloads.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: Agents need multiple operating systems (Linux, Windows, macOS) to access legacy enterprise software. Windows sandboxes spin up in ~1 second (vs. 3–5 minutes for EC2), unlocking RPA-style knowledge work automation on non-API systems.
actionable: When evaluating agent platforms for enterprise workflows, require Windows/macOS support. API-only agents cannot automate ~50% of knowledge work locked in legacy UI-driven systems.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: macOS licensing restrictions (max 2 parallel VMs per machine, 24-hour user-change licensing requirement) make agent-scale macOS sandboxes economically unviable today. Snapshots cannot migrate between physical machines, blocking load balancing. Requires clean-room reimplementation.
actionable: For macOS agent sandboxes, plan for 24-hour-chunk pricing and single-physical-machine persistence. Do not assume feature parity with Linux/Windows sandbox infrastructure.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: CLI access (not MCP APIs alone) dramatically increases agent utility. Agents using shell commands can analyze data, run scripts, and produce downstream results; MCP is a layer of indirection. This distinction mirrors agentic search vs. RAG.
actionable: Prioritize terminal/CLI access in agent sandboxes. Expose CLI tools and scripts alongside or before MCP integrations for higher agent capability and real-time data analysis.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: Knowledge work market (5 largest sectors: healthcare, government, financial services, etc.) represents ~$25 trillion in US wages. If agents automate 40% of this work, the compute market alone could reach $10 trillion annually—vastly larger than current cloud markets.
actionable: Understand the full TAM: CPU compute for agents, not just model inference. The bottleneck is not GPU availability but CPU+RAM allocation capacity.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: Customers using agent-first coding agents (lovable, Cognition, open hands) avoid VCS overhead by snapshotting entire codebases to S3 as JSON on each iteration—no diffs. This indicates Git/GitHub are not designed for agent inner-loop workflows and a new version control primitive is needed.
actionable: When building agent coding platforms, do not force Git workflows. Explore snapshot + searchable JSON versioning for agent-driven iteration. GitHub is optimized for post-laptop CI/CD, not inner-loop agent collaboration.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: High-throughput PR generation (1,000 PRs/day) is bottlenecked by CI capacity, not VCS. Sandboxes can serve as GitHub Actions runners. This opens an opportunity to replace or bypass traditional CI/CD for agent-driven development.
actionable: For high-throughput agent development platforms, use fast sandboxes as CI runners instead of GitHub Actions or equivalent. Avoid queuing on traditional CI bottlenecks.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: SaaS vendors reselling model tokens without exposing underlying APIs/data are not experiencing true revenue acceleration—they lack the margins, stickiness, and usage upside of real API-first platforms. Token consumption models fail when data remains siloed.
actionable: Build API-first, consumption-based SaaS for agents, not token-wrapper layers. Expose underlying data and systems via APIs; charge per agent action, not per model token consumed.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: Dynamic memory resizing in agent sandboxes prevents out-of-memory crashes—a rare feature almost impossible in Kubernetes. This is table-stakes for long-running agent workloads with variable memory demand.
actionable: Require dynamic RAM resizing capability in agent sandbox providers. This prevents costly RL failures and improves reliability vs. statically-provisioned container orchestration.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: For B2B SaaS targeting agents (not end developers), revenue scales faster than PLG. Enterprise customers prioritize responsiveness and support over feature parity. Slack-first responsiveness (5-min huddles, always-on) is a top differentiator vs. feature breadth.
actionable: When building agent infrastructure as B2B SaaS, staff for 24/7 Slack + call responsiveness. Support speed outweighs marginal feature additions in customer retention and pricing power.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: Open-source infrastructure (Apache 2.0 core + AGPL3 sandbox) accelerates cloud product adoption by giving agents visibility into implementation details. Open code provides context to agents during integration, boosting adoption more than docs alone.
actionable: For agent-first platforms, open-source core logic. Agents can read and reason about your SDK code, improving integration success and adoption velocity.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: Future AI infrastructure cloud may look more like Stripe (API-first, developer experience, consumption-based) than AWS (region/resource management complexity). Success requires a cohesive agent-native cloud combining sandboxes, databases, web search, and new primitives yet to be invented.
actionable: When designing agent infrastructure platforms, prioritize Stripe-like simplicity (single API, clear pricing) over AWS-like flexibility. Build a cohesive stack, not a menu of half-integrated services.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: Most enterprise automation (knowledge work) is trapped in legacy Windows/macOS UIs with no APIs exposed. Agents cannot complete these tasks via headless execution alone; they need computer-use (GUI/mouse/keyboard) capabilities to add 10+ trillion dollars of addressable market.
actionable: Prioritize computer-use capabilities for agents targeting enterprise knowledge work. GUI-driven automation unlocks vastly more market than API-first or CLI-only approaches.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: Declarative image building (agent specifies dependencies, system builds snapshot on-the-fly) enables efficient multi-run RL workloads. Agents can modify snapshots between runs without re-provisioning base environments.
actionable: Implement declarative dependency specification for agent environments. Allow agents to request installed packages/tools and auto-snapshot, enabling rapid iteration for RL workflows.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: Pricing should separate CPU (most expensive), RAM, disk, and NOT charge for network. This mirrors GPU compute economics but inverts cost hierarchy. Charging for network invokes unexpected bill shocks and customer churn.
actionable: For agent sandbox pricing, charge per CPU-second and RAM-hour. Do not itemize network costs or disk until unavoidable. Network becomes material at scale and requires separate strategy.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona

claim: Sandbox mean utilization of 15% with 90% peak is sustainable at scale via reserved capacity + commits, but capacity planning is non-linear (spiky RL + predictable background agents). This is a fundamentally new infrastructure operations problem unsolved in traditional cloud.
actionable: When scaling agent compute platforms, expect 15% baseline utilization with 6x peak spikes. Reserve capacity and negotiate multi-customer commitments. Do not plan for typical cloud utilization curves.
source_title: AI Agents Need Computers: 74% MoM Growth, 850K/Day Runs, & New Agent Cloud — Ivan Burazin, Daytona
